title: The neuroscience of dissociation and why it is not what you think
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
You are not broken. You are dissociating.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office, staring at a spot on the wall. She asked me how I felt. I said "fine." And I meant it. I felt nothing. No sadness, no anger, no joy. Just a flat, hollow quiet. Like someone had turned down the volume on my entire life.

She told me I was dissociating. And I thought she meant I was broken. That something was deeply wrong with me. That I was defective in a way other people weren't.

Here's the thing. That is exactly what dissociation wants you to think.

Dissociation is not a sign of weakness. It is not a character flaw. It is not something you chose or something you deserve. It is a survival strategy. Your brain's most creative, most desperate attempt to keep you alive when staying present would have destroyed you.

Stay with me here. Because what I am about to say might shift everything you think you know about your own mind.

What dissociation actually is

Most people think dissociation means "checking out" or "spacing out." And sure, that is part of it. But it is so much more than that. It is a whole-body, whole-brain response to overwhelm. It is the nervous system's emergency brake.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, describes dissociation as the most primitive survival response we have. It is older than fight or flight. It is older than our mammalian brain. It is the response of the oldest part of our nervous system, the dorsal vagal pathway.

Think about it. When a mouse is caught by a cat, it goes limp. It plays dead. Its body shuts down. Its heart rate drops. Its breathing slows. It goes numb. That is not the mouse being lazy. That is the mouse's last-ditch effort to survive. If the cat thinks the mouse is already dead, the cat might lose interest. The mouse lives.

You are not a mouse. But your nervous system does not know the difference between a predator and a traumatic memory. It uses the same ancient wiring.

Dissociation is not a choice. It is a reflex. A deeply intelligent one.

When your system detects that you cannot fight, cannot flee, and cannot freeze in a way that keeps you safe, it goes dorsal. It numbs you. It separates you from your body. It makes you feel like you are watching your life from a great distance. Because if you could feel what was happening to you, it would be too much. You would not survive it.

Wild, right? Your brain literally decided that the only way to keep you alive was to make you disappear.

And here is the part that hurts. That same mechanism that saved you as a child can become a prison as an adult. The system that protected you from unbearable pain can also keep you from feeling joy, from connecting with others, from living a full life. It does not know the threat is gone. It is still running the old program.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana, who works closely with Porges, wrote it. She explains how to recognize when your system is in dorsal mode and how to gently guide it back to safety. It is not about forcing yourself to feel. It is about showing your nervous system that it is okay to come back.

The body keeps the score, even when you don't

Here is something people do not talk about enough. You can dissociate and still have physical symptoms. Your body remembers even when your mind checks out. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent his career documenting this.

You might have chronic pain that no doctor can explain. Migraines. Digestive issues. Fatigue. You might feel restless, agitated, on edge. Or you might feel nothing at all. A strange hollow emptiness in your chest. A sense that you are not really here.

These are not separate problems. They are the same problem. Your nervous system is stuck in a survival state. And dissociation is how it keeps you from feeling the full weight of that stuckness.

I spent years thinking I had a medical condition. I saw specialists. I had tests. Everything came back normal. And I felt crazy. Because something was clearly wrong, but nobody could find it. Nobody told me that my nervous system could be the source. Nobody told me that dissociation could look like this.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, wrote it. He explains how the body holds trauma and how gentle, titrated movement can release it. It is not about reliving the trauma. It is about completing the survival response that got stuck.

Notice what just happened in your body. When I said "completing the survival response," did you feel something shift? A small flutter? A sense of recognition? That is your system listening. That is hope.

Why dissociation gets misdiagnosed

Here is where it gets complicated. Dissociation looks like a lot of other things. It can look like depression. It can look like anxiety. It can look like ADHD. It can look like a personality disorder. And because most clinicians are not trained to recognize it, it gets missed. Constantly.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist and author, has written extensively about this. She says that many people who are diagnosed with borderline personality disorder are actually dissociative. Their mood swings, their identity confusion, their difficulty with relationships - these are not personality flaws. They are the aftermath of severe, chronic trauma. They are the fragmented parts of a self that had to split apart to survive.

If you have been told you have a personality disorder, and something about that diagnosis never felt right, you are not alone. I have sat with dozens of people who were told they were "too sensitive" or "dramatic" or "manipulative." They were not any of those things. They were dissociating. And nobody had the language to explain it to them.

Think about how damaging that is. You go to a professional for help. You tell them you feel disconnected, unreal, numb. And they tell you there is something wrong with your character. They tell you to try harder. They tell you to practice mindfulness. And you try. God, you try. But mindfulness when you are dissociating can make it worse. Because you are not grounded enough to stay present. You just dissociate harder.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work on inherited family trauma explains how dissociation can pass down through generations. It is not just your story. It is your grandmother's story. And her mother's. The nervous system remembers what the mind has forgotten.

How to come back without forcing it

I am not going to tell you to "just feel your feelings." That is toxic positivity dressed up as wisdom. If you are dissociating, you cannot feel your feelings. That is the whole point. Your system is protecting you from feelings it thinks will kill you. Telling you to feel them is like telling someone with a broken leg to run a marathon.

What works is slower. Gentler. More respectful of your system's wisdom.

Start with the edges. Notice one thing in the room. The color of the wall. The texture of the fabric on your chair. The sound of your own breath. Do not try to feel anything. Just notice. That is enough.

Then notice something else. And something else. Tiny anchors. Each one a small thread pulling you back into the present moment. Not by force. By invitation.

Your system needs to know it is safe before it will come back online. And safety is not a concept. It is a felt experience. It is the weight of your body in the chair. The cool air on your skin. The steady rhythm of your heart. You have to show your nervous system, not tell it.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It is an amino acid that promotes relaxation without sedation. It can help take the edge off the hypervigilance that often precedes dissociation. It is not a cure. Nothing is. But it can make the process a little easier.

I am not going to tell you that recovery is linear. It is not. You will have days when you feel present, alive, connected. And you will have days when you feel like a ghost in your own life. That is not failure. That is the nature of healing. Your system is learning a new way of being. That takes time. That takes patience. That takes a kind of compassion most of us were never taught.

Sit with that. You are not broken. You are not too far gone. You are a person whose nervous system did exactly what it was supposed to do. And now, with the right support, it can learn something new.

The door is not locked. It was never locked. You just had to find the right key.
